title: Android ViewModel – Persistent UI State Management

meta.title: Android ViewModel Guide – Lifecycle, State Restoration & Best Practices (2026)
meta.description: Master the Android ViewModel component. Learn how to persist UI state during configuration changes and separate business logic from UI in native Android.

content.value: In the Android Architecture Components, the ViewModel's primary job is to store and manage UI-related data. Its unique power lies in its **Lifecycle Awareness**: it remains in memory during an Activity's destruction and recreation (e.g., rotation) and only clears itself when the Activity is actually finished. For an Engineering Manager, this is the key to 'Separation of Concerns'—keeping the Activity focused on rendering while the ViewModel handles the data logic.

When an Activity is recreated, its local variables are wiped. To prevent Revochamp from restarting an AI generation process every time the user tilts their phone, we shift the 'Generation Status' into the ViewModel. In 2026, we utilize **Kotlin Coroutines** inside the `viewModelScope` to ensure that background tasks are automatically canceled if the user leaves the app entirely.

While ViewModels survive rotation, they do not survive **Process Death** (when the OS kills the app in the background to save RAM). To handle this, we use `SavedStateHandle`. This is a key-value map that persists through process death, allowing the user to return to their work in Chennai's humid outdoor conditions even if their mid-range device cleared the app from memory.

Fragments within the same Activity can share a single ViewModel. This is a common pattern for 'Master-Detail' views in Revochamp, where one fragment updates a value and another fragment observes that change instantly, without the need for complex interface callbacks.

faq.question: Can I pass a Context into a ViewModel?
faq.answer: Never pass an Activity or View context into a ViewModel. This will cause a memory leak because the ViewModel outlives the Activity. If you need an application-level context, use 'AndroidViewModel', which provides a reference to the Application context safely.

faq.question: How do I instantiate a ViewModel?
faq.answer: You should never call the constructor directly (e.g., 'val vm = MyViewModel()'). Instead, use 'ViewModelProvider' or the 'by viewModels()' Kotlin delegate. This allows the system to check if a ViewModel instance already exists before creating a new one.

faq.question: When is onCleared() called?
faq.answer: 'onCleared()' is called right before the ViewModel is destroyed. This is the perfect place to manually close sockets, cancel observers, or release heavy resources that aren't tied to the viewModelScope.

quiz.question: What happens to a ViewModel when the screen is rotated?
options: It remains in memory and is re-attached to the new Activity
It is paused and then resumed

quiz.question: Which component is used to persist ViewModel data specifically through system-initiated process death?
Why should you NOT store a reference to an Activity inside a ViewModel?
options: It is too much data for the ViewModel
It will cause a memory leak when the Activity is destroyed
The compiler will prevent it automatically
